title: Term Sheet Literacy Challenge

introduction.subtitle: Knowledge quiz. 8-10 min.
introduction.description:
Term Sheet Literacy Challenge is a learning challenge for the terminology, assumptions, and typical decision traps behind Economics & Dilution, Control & Governance, Downside Protection, Process & Rights. It is designed to teach through plausible wrong answers as much as through the correct ones.

Choose the answer you would use in a real conversation. After completion, the answer review explains the concept and points to the area worth studying next.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: An investor invests EUR 2 million at an EUR 8 million pre-money valuation. Before option-pool or convertible adjustments, what is the investor post-money ownership?

options.text: D. It cannot be calculated without the exit value.

conceptHelp.title: Economics & Dilution: An investor invests EUR 2 million at an EUR 8 million pre-money valuation. Before option-pool or convertible adjustments, what is the investor post-money ownership
conceptHelp.summary: Economics & Dilution means how valuation, option pools, share price, and liquidation economics affect founder and investor ownership outcomes. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "An investor invests EUR 2 million at an EUR 8 million pre-money valuation. Before option-pool or convertible adjustments, what is the investor post-money ownership?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.

questions.text: What is the usual economic effect of increasing a pre-money option pool immediately before the financing?
questions.explanation: a pre-money pool top-up primarily dilutes existing shareholders.

options.text: A. It primarily dilutes existing shareholders.
B. It primarily dilutes the incoming investor.
C. It has no dilution effect until options are exercised.
D. It automatically increases the pre-money valuation.

conceptHelp.title: Economics & Dilution: What is the usual economic effect of increasing a pre-money option pool immediately before the financing
conceptHelp.summary: Economics & Dilution means how valuation, option pools, share price, and liquidation economics affect founder and investor ownership outcomes. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "What is the usual economic effect of increasing a pre-money option pool immediately before the financing?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "It primarily dilutes existing shareholders."

questions.text: Why can board composition matter even when founders still own a majority of shares?
questions.explanation: board rights can control key corporate decisions.

options.text: A. Boards approve only annual accounts, so composition rarely matters.
B. The board may control key corporate decisions, appointments and strategic approvals.
C. A board seat automatically transfers share ownership.
D. Board composition matters only after an IPO.

conceptHelp.title: Control & Governance: Why can board composition matter even when founders still own a majority of shares
conceptHelp.summary: Control & Governance means the rights, board structure, approvals, and vetoes that shape who can decide after investment. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "Why can board composition matter even when founders still own a majority of shares?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "The board may control key corporate decisions, appointments and strategic approvals."

questions.text: What are protective provisions generally designed to do?
questions.explanation: protective provisions give consent rights over specified fundamental actions.

options.text: A. Give investors consent rights over specified fundamental actions.
B. Guarantee that the investment will produce a return.
C. Replace all duties of the board.
D. Prevent employees from receiving options.

conceptHelp.title: Control & Governance: What are protective provisions generally designed to do
conceptHelp.summary: Control & Governance means the rights, board structure, approvals, and vetoes that shape who can decide after investment. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "What are protective provisions generally designed to do?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Give investors consent rights over specified fundamental actions."

questions.text: What does a 1x non-participating liquidation preference generally provide?
questions.explanation: non-participating preferred chooses preference amount or as-converted proceeds.

options.text: A. The investor receives both the original investment and full common-share participation in every case.
B. The investor typically chooses the greater of the preference amount or the as-converted common proceeds.
C. The investor receives one percent of the company.
D. The founders must repay the investor personally.

conceptHelp.title: Downside Protection: What does a 1x non-participating liquidation preference generally provide
conceptHelp.summary: Downside Protection means terms that protect investors in poor outcomes and can change who receives value at exit or down rounds. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "What does a 1x non-participating liquidation preference generally provide?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "The investor typically chooses the greater of the preference amount or the as-converted common proceeds."

questions.text: Compared with broad-based weighted-average anti-dilution, full-ratchet anti-dilution is generally what?
questions.explanation: full ratchet can reset conversion price to the new lower price regardless of round size.

options.text: A. Less dilutive to existing common holders.
B. More punitive because it can reset the conversion price to the new lower price regardless of round size.
C. Identical in all financing scenarios.
D. Relevant only to employee options.

conceptHelp.title: Downside Protection: Compared with broad-based weighted-average anti-dilution, full-ratchet anti-dilution is generally what
conceptHelp.summary: Downside Protection means terms that protect investors in poor outcomes and can change who receives value at exit or down rounds. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "Compared with broad-based weighted-average anti-dilution, full-ratchet anti-dilution is generally what?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "More punitive because it can reset the conversion price to the new lower price regardless of round size."

questions.text: What does a pro-rata right generally allow an investor to do?
questions.explanation: pro-rata rights allow participation to maintain ownership subject to the agreement.

options.text: A. Maintain a proportionate ownership stake in a future financing, subject to the agreement.
B. Force the company to repurchase shares at any time.
C. Approve every customer contract.
D. Receive a fixed dividend before all creditors.

conceptHelp.title: Process & Rights: What does a pro-rata right generally allow an investor to do
conceptHelp.summary: Process & Rights means the investor rights and deal-process clauses that shape access, follow-on participation, exclusivity, and closing behavior. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "What does a pro-rata right generally allow an investor to do?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Maintain a proportionate ownership stake in a future financing, subject to the agreement."

questions.text: What is the practical purpose of a no-shop or exclusivity clause?
questions.explanation: exclusivity restricts competing financing discussions for a defined period.

options.text: A. It restricts the company from soliciting or negotiating competing financing offers for a defined period.
B. It guarantees that the financing will close.
C. It prevents the company from selling products.
D. It removes the need for definitive legal documents.

conceptHelp.title: Process & Rights: What is the practical purpose of a no-shop or exclusivity clause
conceptHelp.summary: Process & Rights means the investor rights and deal-process clauses that shape access, follow-on participation, exclusivity, and closing behavior. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "What is the practical purpose of a no-shop or exclusivity clause?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "It restricts the company from soliciting or negotiating competing financing offers for a defined period."

questions.text: Why does a pre-money option-pool top-up change founder dilution?
questions.explanation: A pre-money option pool is usually created before the new investment is priced, so existing holders bear most of that dilution.

options.text: A. Because it is usually created before the new money comes in, diluting existing holders.
B. Because it always dilutes only the incoming investor.
C. Because it removes the need for future hiring grants.
D. Because it converts preferred shares into debt.

conceptHelp.title: Economics & Dilution: Why does a pre-money option-pool top-up change founder dilution
conceptHelp.summary: Economics & Dilution is tested here through a concrete operating question: "Why does a pre-money option-pool top-up change founder dilution?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Because it is usually created before the new money comes in, diluting existing holders."

questions.text: What is the cleanest founder-friendly way to negotiate investor veto rights?
questions.explanation: Reserved matters should be specific and material so normal operating decisions are not blocked unnecessarily.

options.text: A. Reject every veto right, including those over issuing senior securities.
B. Define narrow reserved matters tied to material company actions.
C. Accept broad vetoes and rely on goodwill later.
D. Hide important decisions from the board.

conceptHelp.title: Control & Governance: What is the cleanest founder-friendly way to negotiate investor veto rights
conceptHelp.summary: Control & Governance is tested here through a concrete operating question: "What is the cleanest founder-friendly way to negotiate investor veto rights?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Define narrow reserved matters tied to material company actions."

questions.text: What does uncapped participating preferred generally allow in a sale?
questions.explanation: Participating preferred can take the liquidation preference first and then participate in remaining proceeds pro rata, unless capped or negotiated otherwise.

options.text: A. The investor gets only common-share treatment.
B. The investor takes the preference and then also participates pro rata in remaining proceeds.
C. The investor loses the liquidation preference if the company sells below valuation.
D. The founders always receive the first proceeds.

conceptHelp.title: Downside Protection: What does uncapped participating preferred generally allow in a sale
conceptHelp.summary: Downside Protection is tested here through a concrete operating question: "What does uncapped participating preferred generally allow in a sale?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "The investor takes the preference and then also participates pro rata in remaining proceeds."

questions.text: What should founders clarify before agreeing to exclusivity during financing?
questions.explanation: Exclusivity is less dangerous when timeline, diligence scope, deliverables, and fallback options are explicit.

options.text: A. Only the press announcement date.
B. Timeline, diligence scope, closing conditions, and what happens if the investor stalls.
C. Nothing; exclusivity has no practical effect.
D. Only whether the investor likes the logo.

conceptHelp.title: Process & Rights: What should founders clarify before agreeing to exclusivity during financing
conceptHelp.summary: Process & Rights is tested here through a concrete operating question: "What should founders clarify before agreeing to exclusivity during financing?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Timeline, diligence scope, closing conditions, and what happens if the investor stalls."

questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. The quiz is meant to teach the vocabulary before it tests recall.
